behaviors.description: Renamed files appear in their own category and are not also counted as a deletion plus an addition.
behaviors.justification: Failure can come from real `git` rename detection/flags and subprocess output shape, not just local categorization logic. A stubbed diff/status response could hide bugs where the CLI invokes the wrong git command/options and never receives rename entries.
behaviors.test_description: Create a real temp repo, commit a file, rename it (`git mv`), run the CLI as a subprocess, and assert report has the file only under `renamed` with no corresponding `added`/`deleted` increment.

behaviors.description: Diffs that include binary files do not cause the tool to crash; binary files are counted in the output.
behaviors.justification: Binary diff edge cases depend on real git subprocess behavior and real file content handling. Mocked text fixtures can miss crashes caused by actual git binary diff output/exit behavior.
behaviors.test_description: In a real temp repo, add and modify a binary file (e.g., random bytes), invoke the CLI subprocess, assert zero crash/exit success and that binary change is reflected in the relevant count/category.

behaviors.description: Running the tool on a repository that has no commits yet produces an empty report rather than an error.
behaviors.justification: This behavior hinges on real git command failures/exit codes in an unborn-HEAD repository. In-process fakes may not reproduce exact subprocess stderr/return-code paths that trigger crashes.
behaviors.test_description: Initialize an empty git repo with no commits, run the CLI subprocess from that repo path, and assert successful execution plus an empty structured report.

behaviors.description: The counts in the report match the actual change history of the repository being analyzed.
behaviors.justification: The core risk is mismatch between real repository history and what the CLI derives via git subprocesses. This is an end-to-end infra boundary (filesystem + git process), so lower-level mocks can hide real history interpretation failures.
behaviors.test_description: Programmatically build a real repo history containing added/deleted/renamed/modified files across commits, run the CLI subprocess, and compare reported counts to independently computed expected counts from known operations.

behaviors.description: The output is identical whether the changes being analyzed are staged or unstaged.
behaviors.justification: Staged vs unstaged semantics are defined by git index/working-tree behavior, which requires real git state transitions and subprocess calls. Mocking usually hides index-specific edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: In a real repo, create the same net file changes in two scenarios (staged and unstaged), run CLI subprocess for each scenario, and assert byte-for-byte identical structured output.
